category: multi-variant page where the default variant is a small sampler

consistency.notes: All non-null, non-empty, non-'unknown' labels are supported by the page text. The title, roaster, footer location, origin, region, process, variety, producer/farm, altitude, roast level, harvest period, tasting notes, descriptive text, default 100g sampler size, USD price of 7.50, package grams, one-time purchase context, and availability are all explicitly shown. 'coffee.roaster_country' = USA is supported indirectly by 'Seattle, WA'. 'coffee.is_espresso' = false is supported by 'Not roasted for espresso.' 'coffee.is_blend' = false follows from the product being labeled 'Single Origin'. 'coffee.is_decaf' = false is supported by the named caffeinated coffee product with no decaf indication. 'coffee.is_coferment_or_infused' = false is supported by the standard washed-process description with no co-ferment/infusion mention. 'is_specialty_coffee' is supported by the detailed single-origin specialty-style listing.

realism.reasons: Very plausible as an html-to-text rendering of a real specialty coffee product page. The structure is exactly what you'd expect from a Shopify-style roaster page: header/nav, breadcrumb-like category line, title, starting price, size options, add-to-cart, descriptive copy, shipping note, related products, embedded variant data, and footer. The voice is on-brand for specialty coffee—concise, sensory, and specific without overexplaining. Origin details are coherent and realistic: producer name, farm, Palestina/Huila, Pink Bourbon, washed process, altitude band, and harvest window all fit current Colombian specialty coffee conventions. The processing description also reads credibly detailed rather than random. Pricing across 100g / 250g / 1kg is believable for a premium Pink Bourbon release, and the sampler note pinned to 100g feels like a genuine ecommerce merchandising detail. The variant block is internally consistent, with sensible SKUs, names, weights, and availability fields. Minor tiny tells only: ‘Not roasted for espresso’ is a little more explicit than some roasters use, and ‘default_for_inference’ in the variant block looks like an extraction artifact rather than customer-facing data—but both are fully compatible with a real html-to-text scrape rather than evidence of fakery.

label.coffee.producer_or_farm: Maria Fernanda Arias, Finca La Estrella
label.coffee.sensory_text:
Tasting notes: blood orange, panela, jasmine

A bright and deeply sweet Huila coffee from Maria Fernanda Arias, whose family farm sits above Palestina on the eastern slopes of the Magdalena valley. The cup opens with blood orange and jasmine aromatics, then settles into panela sweetness and a clean black-tea finish.
label.coffee.producer_text:
A bright and deeply sweet Huila coffee from Maria Fernanda Arias, whose family farm sits above Palestina on the eastern slopes of the Magdalena valley. The cup opens with blood orange and jasmine aromatics, then settles into panela sweetness and a clean black-tea finish.

For this lot, ripe Pink Bourbon cherries were hand-sorted, depulped the same afternoon, fermented in tiled tanks for 30 hours, and washed with cool spring water before slow drying on raised beds. We roasted it lightly to preserve the floral structure and citrus clarity.
